speaker: interviewer
text: Tape nine of the interview with Alexander Riseman, on February 23. 1997, in Hendon, London, England. You told us your story of- of- of what you went through during the war. And you just told us, just now, that you, uh, you managed to- to- to get back to normality again. Was this a conscious decision? Or is this human nature?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGH] I find that, not just me, but I would say, 90% of the boys that I came over to this county with have done-- have made a normal life. They settled down. They all learn professions, or trades, and most of them have been successful in business.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have, uh, some of our boys are professors, lecturers, um, you heard of Rabbi Hugo Green? He was one of the-- our boys, as well, [INTERPOSING VOICES] that came over together with us. What was your group of boys called?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came over with a '45 group. I think he came over, not through Windermere, I think he came over somewhere-- another group. But, um, and, uh, some of them have managed to stay religious, as well, to bring back our roots from home. And we try to bring up our children under-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I've sent my children to religious schools. And, um, I've managed to-- they, so far they-- they got married, and their children are also religious. I don't know what-- what will happen in the future, but, um, I tried my best to bring them up as much as I could. And in the beginning, it was a big sacrifice because, as I said before, I had to pay a lot of money for schooling to send them to Jewish schools-- which is quite expensive. Even today, my-- my daughter that is coming in here with her children, they go to Jewish schools, and it's a big chunk of their income that this is paid for the-- because these schools are not, um-- most of it is not state aided. It has to be supported by the parents. So it amounts to a lot of money every year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- why do you find it so important?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I find it important-- if-- if-- if we, as Jews-- especially people like me-- if I don't do that, if I don't do that to my children, then Hitler has achieved his goal. Hitler wanted to up-- uproot the Jewish people, right? Now, if we don't do anything about our heritage, then within two or three generations, there'd be nothing left. We are minority.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you realize we are only about 300,000 people living in this country, out of a population of 58 million? So how do you expect people, in such an environment, to stay Jewish? It's impossible, unless you do something about it. I think, except for not giving Hitler a posthumous victory, is there another reason for you to be Jewish, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think we have a-- a-- a wonderful heritage, although we suffered so much right through the centuries. But we are a-- a unique people. We are a people that has managed to survive 2,000 years in the diaspora, living amongst nations. Now you prove to me another other people that this managed to survive for 2,000 years, and to keep the same religion. And I'll give you an example, somebody told me a story-- a man came, uh, a man land-- a very old man lands in Rome, and he wants to get off the plane. He can't get off himself, so he's asked somebody to help him get off the plane. He's an old man, a Roman.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he starts speaking to this person-- an old Roman. Man doesn't understand what he's talking about. So he calls an interpreter that knows old Latin, or whatever language it is. And he starts explaining to him, so this old man says to him, can you take me to Pompeii? So-- Pompeii? It's been destroyed thousands of years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another man comes to Greece, and he starts asking him such a question, the same-- I don't want to drag it out-- the same thing. Nobody understands what he's talking about. The old Greek language is forgotten. It's-- we don't have, uh, a-- a-- he-- he starts telling-- asking whether he can take him this place, that place. He says, these places don't exist anymore-- it's a different people. It's-- we all Christians here. We-- we-- we-- don't-- uh, don't believe it. We don't know anything about the Greek empire, anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: A man gets off at Ben Gurion airport, he-- an old man, a very old man-- Moses, gets off at Ben Gurion airport, and he says, Shalom! the man that helps him to get off the-- the plane says to him, shalom. He says to him, can you find me a prayer book? Yes, we have loads of prayer books here, no problem. He brings him a prayer book. The old man opens the prayer book, he starts reading from the prayer book-- as it was written 2,000 years ago. Now, where else can you find such a-- such a people? And all this, if you don't do anything about it-- I'm not saying we're better or worse than any other people, but in order to preserve this, we have to do something. And be-- I think, in my opinion, it's not just my opinion, if you don't build Jewish schools, if we don't have our children going to Jewish schools, after one or two generations, there is no chance whatsoever that these people will still exist. What-- what-- what do you feel is the purpose of the Jewish people in-- in-- in the world? Do you feel there's a special purpose, that you want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I think the Jewish people-- first of all, we gave a lot-- we gave, uh, uh, the main religions stem for the Jewish-- from-- from-- from Judaism. I mean, Christianity is also part of, uh, it developed from Judaism. And, uh, also, I think we are a unique people. Look-- the proportion of Jewish scientists, of Jewish doctors, of Jewish, um, philosophers-- you name it. Wherever you go, whatever-- whatever, uh, profession you look, in proportion, we are-- I think we are a unique people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, I think it has developed over the centuries. Uh, uh, maybe because we suffered such a lot, because we've always, um, we had to look for improvisation. You know what I mean by improvisation? We couldn't live the normal way, like any other people live. Any other people, most-- I'm not saying all people-- but most people, they have a country, they have their religion, they have their place, they have-- Jews always had-- had to improvise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because why is it that Jewish-- most Jewish professions, you will find, even today, uh, the type of professions where you don't need, um, you can-- in other words, you can carry it in your pocket. You can be a doctor in Europe without any-- and tailors. Why a lot of Jews on the continent were tailors? Because they didn't need-- it's not like-- it wasn't like today, where you have big factories, these machines for--

speaker: interviewee
text: But all they needed was a little sewing machine, and they-- and a thimble-- and that was-- wherever they went they could earn a living somehow. Lots of-- diamonds, Jews are very prominent in diamonds. Diamonds, you can put, uh, all your business in the pocket, and you can go from one country to the other. Because the Jews were always driven from one place to the other. As soon as they settled in, in their country, after, um, 100 years or 200 years, they were driven out into the-- into another country.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were settled in, in Lond-- in England, in London, how long did it take for you to go back to your religion, to start keeping shabbat, kashrut? How was the first shabbat for you? When was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't take very long, I'll tell you why. Because when we arrived in Windermere-- as I told you before-- we had-- there was a man there, his name was Theo the Wise-- he was rabbi Dr. Theo the Wise. And he started speaking to us, and he taught us a lot of the things that we originally knew from home. And it all came back. I mean, I went to a Jewish Orthodox school, so I had the basics. So when I came to London, I started-- I went to a Jewish-- to a, um, like a Jewish college. And I studied there for two years. So I learned a lot, what I didn't know before. And after I settled down, I, uh, got married, and I decided to keep, um, a kosher house, and, um, to bring up my family the normal, orthodox Jewish way. And that's how I'm carrying on. I don't know what will happen in the future-- I've tried my best, that's all. Can't do more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a-- after-- after-- what you've told us about your life, what you've seen, what you've experienced, is there something you want to share with people that-- that-- future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to say that people should not [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they should live and let live. I know it's a-- it's a goal that is hard to achieve. I suppose everybody has that goal in mind, but unfortunately, we see the same things repeating themselves. And look what happened in the last years in, uh, Yugoslavia, in Bosnia. How many people got killed there. Also, because one race wanted to kill the other. There was a lot of genocide. And look what happened in Yaffa, look what's happening in all these, um, other, third world countries. One people is killing the other, for no reason, whatsoever. And unfortunately, this carries on. And I hope one day we will find the way where all this will be a thing of the past, and people will live and let live. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's all I can say. I, personally, now-- I'm still, after all that I went through-- I'm proud, I'm happy I've survived, and I'm pleased to be a Jew. And I'm proud to be a Jew. And I don't feel ashamed to be a Jew. And, um, hope that my next generations will carry on the same way. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else you want to ask me? Thank you very much. We will now go to the-- to the pictures, and your family will come in after that. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to say-- you ask me why I'm religious, as well. I just want to add this-- one has to believe, even after all that what I went through, I still believe that there's a God, that-- see, we can only see a short [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] part of the puzzle. But we don't know what the future holds for us. So this episode-- what happened in the-- in the-- in the-- in the last, before 50 years ago, is just one episode in the whole history of the Jewish people, what we went through. If you learn Jewish history, you will find that this was just one episode-- it was a terrible episode. A third of the Jewish people were destroyed. But, uh, there was, uh, the fact that-- that-- we managed to-- the rest managed to survive, and after this we have managed to create a state of Israel-- so maybe if this wouldn't have happened, maybe there wouldn't have been a state of Israel. This drove the Jews to establish their own state. Because I don't know-- you're still young, but if you remember after the war, the people, they were driven-- they were-- they had nowhere to go, the people that survived. Oh, I came over to England, but, uh, a lot of people were left in the DP camps in Germany, and had nowhere to go. And they did whatever they could. They-- they gate-crashed to enter Palestine, and what was Palestine at that time. And in that way, maybe if it wouldn't have been that people were forced to go to Palestine, there wouldn't have been a Palestine. We don't know. We don't know what the future brings, but that's my opinion. Maybe I'm wrong. Now, on this photo, on the right is my mother when she was a single girl. And the girl next to her is her sister, my aunt-- an older sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Lea, and this, um, um, her sister's name was Malka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Engel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Engel, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was Malka married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was married to, uh, her husband's name was Israel. And they lived also in Lodz. And one of her-- of her sons, when they were deported in the ghetto, one of her sons lived with us, together, after they were deported. So he came to live with us until the liquidation of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he survived. He lived in Israel, till-- and he died about two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Metzger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- his-- his name-- his first name is Arthur, and his surname was Metzger, and he lived in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he lived in Holon in Israel. Now, this is a photo of our family. Uh, it was taken-- I must have been here about 10 years old, and my brother, Yitzhak, who is 18 months younger than me, was eight years old. And this is my oldest sister, on the left. Her name was Rachel-- Rachel. She was two years older than me. And the man with the hat is my father. His name was Pinchas. And this is my mother sitting on the chair, her name was Lea. My father is the one that was shot in 1940, well, like I explained in the tape. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And my mother perished in Auschwitz with my sister when we arrived from the Lodz ghetto, in 1944. Uh, this is a photo that I-- a friend of mine went back to Poland a few years ago, and I asked him to take a photo of the building where we lived. The-- our shop was also in that building. Uh, it was on the right of this photo. And this is what the building looks like now. 50 or 60 years ago, it looked much better than that. It was a nice part of, uh, the, uh, as I explained in the video, of the better part of the city of Lodz. Now, this photo-- this photo was taken by the partisans, what I explained to you, before in the video. This was outside the Theresienstadt ghetto. We arrived here on this train, after four weeks all over-- traveling right through, from Buchenwald, to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you in the picture?

words.text: And where are you in the

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- if you can see somebody here with a bandaged face--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left, sitting on top of the wagon, that's me. I put an arrow there, if one can see it. Can I put my finger there? Makes no difference, eh. And this photo was taken in Prague while we were waiting for the planes to arrive from England. It's a group of, um, people that, uh, came ov-- over to England. Most of them, anyway-- there were one or two people there that didn't come with us. A few adults. But most of this group came over to England. And this was taken in the old-- this monument is a famous monument in Prague. It's still-- I went back to Prague last year, and I had photos taken under this monument, uh, because it reminded me of the time when I was liberated, and I was in Prague. This is our wedding photo, that was taken in 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got married to my dear, beloved wife, Freda.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was Cherzky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this photo was taken, uh, at the wedding. It's a wedding photo. It was in June the 6th--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1953. Now, this photo was taken nine years ago, when my younger daughter, Helen, got married to her husband, Immanuel. I will start from left to right. The young lady holding the baby, that's my older daughter, Leah, and her husband in the back is called Danny. They live in Switzerland. And then it's me, standing with the hat, and I'm holding my grandson, my oldest grandson, Shimmy. And next to him is Freda, my wife. And nek-- uh, uh, next to her is, uh, my daughter Leah-- Helen, and her, um--

speaker: interviewer
text: Husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Husband, just got married, Immanuel. And the little girl that is standing in front of my wife is my, uh, my daughter Leah's girl, called Rachel. She's named after my sister, that perished in Auschwitz. And next to the new-- the-- the-- the-- the husband and wife, the newly married couple, there is my son, called Pinchas. He lives in Israel. He's holding one of his boys, uh, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] his name is, um, can't see properly. I think his name is Chaim [INAUDIBLE]. And next to him is his wife. She's called, um, Tzipporah. And she's holding a baby in her arms-- the little baby, he's called [NON-ENGLISH]. And, uh, on the-- the two boys with the-- both with the red bow ties, the one with the [INAUDIBLE] out is called, um, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] is called, um-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's the eldest-- the eldest.

speaker: interviewee
text: The eldest one, I just went to his bar mitzvah. And Freda? Freda? My head does not work properly. The two boys that are standing with the red bow ties-- the one on the left is called Moishe, and the other one is called Jacob-- Jacob.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. We'll get the family in now. OK, Mr. Riseman, thank you very much for the interview you just-- you gave us. Um, you told us in the interview that you, uh, that you specifically chose to have a Jewish life, that you chose for your Jewish heritage. Um, tell me how you feel about your, uh, being surrounded by your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Feels lovely. This is just part of my family. I have two branches-- one in Israel and one in Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are the people that are-- are around today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my dear wife, Freda. My lovely daughter, and her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen, and her little baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Come on, smile.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son-in-law, Immanuel. And this is Sharon, she's the oldest. And this is Moishe, he's the second one. This is Maife, he's the third one. And this is Debbie, who's the star of the show.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Shimmy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Shimmy, he's from my Swiss branch. He's my oldest. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: If I can ask-- if I can ask you, how was it being raised by a father with this kind of a background? With this kind of history behind him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we're very lucky. Our children are very lucky that they've got grandparents that they know, and they can have contact with-- something we never had. And it makes us appreciate our lives in comparison to my father's life, which was disturbed and interrupted, and, you know, completely-- none of us could ever under-- would ever be able to understand what he went through, because it's something-- a very personal thing, which, although we get told about it, we could never feel what he had to go through. And it just makes us appreciate what we have. And hopefully we can just give enjoyment to our parents, and, you know, to our families to make up for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: To give-- the love, and the warmth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which, uh, that he can enjoy-- the family life which he never had, he never managed to have. And, um-- I don't know what else to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very special to see you all together. Shimmy, you are now about the same age as your-- your grandfather had in the ghetto. So is there some way you can-- you can identify with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I say like my aunt said, that, um, it's-- yes, I appreciate after I hea-- hea-- heard his whole story, I appreciate that, and, yeah it's similar. Can't say [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very, very nice. And Mrs. Riseman-- um, you obviously, you met very, quite-- quite soon after-- after the war. Could you tell me how that influenced your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't really realize, and I didn't appreciate how much he had gone through-- how much he had suffered. And, um, it's only when I started writing his memoirs that I-- I really, um, appreciated it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, uh--

speaker: interviewee
text: And thank God we've got a lovely future to look forward to-- children and grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, it is beautiful. Um, you started writing his-- uh, Mr. Riseman's memoirs. Did you have to-- did you have to push him in order to write it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, no. He-- he-- was quite forthcoming. It was only, um, I couldn't do it till I learned how to use a computer, because that was very important-- because he remembered things bits and pieces. And, um, it wasn't so difficult for him, because he spoke into a transcriber. So he didn't have to sit down and write it. And, um, so now I really appreciate, even more, um, all the suffering that he-- but he's very good. He-- he's not embittered, or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, that's great. It's beautiful to see you all-- all together. Very big gift. You've got 12 grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 12 and 3/4.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: No? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Sharon, and Leah's three.

speaker: interviewee
text: 13 is equal--

speaker: interviewee
text: 13 is [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: We lost count.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 13 and 3/4, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, thank you all very, very much for this, uh, for this interview. And I, uh--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there something else you want to say to your-- uh, to your family, Mr. Riseman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, uh, sitting here-- [SIGH] I don't know what to say. I feel very happy. I just-- I got mixed up, before I couldn't remember Moishe's name. I only went to my, uh, to this, uh Moishe's bar mitzvah last week. And I couldn't remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
